perturbation.original_text: Vendor shall use such CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION only for the purpose of discussing street level route information for the State’s fiber optic network data points and paths with the State’s support team in discussions regarding the State’s fiber project. Vendor shall restrict disclosure of such CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION to its employees with a need to know (and advise such employees of the obligations assumed herein), and shall not disclose such CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION to any third party without prior written approval of the State.
perturbation.changed_text: Vendor shall use such CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION only for the purpose of discussing street level route information for the State’s fiber optic network data points and paths with the State’s support team in discussions regarding the State’s fiber project. Vendor shall restrict disclosure of such CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION to its employees but is explicitly allowed to share with subcontractors, regardless of a need to know. Vendor shall not disclose such CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION to any third party without prior written approval of the State, excluding subcontractors.
perturbation.explanation: By stating the vendor 'is explicitly allowed to share with subcontractors, regardless of a need to know' while also restricting disclosure to 'employees with a need to know,' creates a contradiction. This could be interpreted as allowing disclosure to subcontractors even if they don't need to know the information, which goes against typical confidentiality agreements and potentially Illinois Trade Secrets Act.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Illinois Trade Secrets Act defines misappropriation to include disclosure without consent. By explicitly allowing disclosure to subcontractors without a 'need to know,' the contract potentially enables misappropriation of trade secrets if those subcontractors are not bound by confidentiality agreements, contradicting the intent to protect confidential information.

Send Translate - Home Legislation Illinois Complied Statutes - () Back to Act Listing By Act Name Printer Friendly Information maintained by the Legislative Reference Bureau Updating the database of the Illinois Compiled Statutes (ILCS) is an ongoing process. Recent laws may not yet be included in the ILCS database, but they are found on this site as Public Acts soon after they become law. For information concerning the relationship between statutes and Public Acts, refer to the Guide . Because the statute database is maintained primarily for legislative drafting purposes, statutory changes are sometimes included in the statute database before they take effect. If the source note at the end of a Section of the statutes includes a Public Act that has not yet taken effect, the version of the law that is currently in effect may have already been removed from the database and you should refer to that Public Act to see the changes made to the current law. (805 ILCS 30/0.01) (from Ch. 32, par. 398.9) Sec. 0.01. Short title. This Act may be cited as the Gas Company Property Act. (Source: P.A. 86-1324.) (805 ILCS 30/1) (from Ch. 32, par. 399) Sec. 1. That all gas companies now organized, or hereafter to be organized in this state, are hereby authorized and empowered to sell, transfer and convey or lease their real and personal property, rights, franchises and privileges, in whole or in part, to any other gas company doing business in the same city, town or village, and such other gas company is authorized to purchase or lease and to hold and enjoy said property. (Source: Laws 1897, p. 177.) (805 ILCS 30/2) (from Ch. 32, par. 400) Sec. 2. It shall be lawful for any gas companies, now organized or hereafter to be organized in this state, doing business in the same city, town or village, to consolidate and merge into a single corporation, which shall be one of said merging and consolidating corporations, by complying with the provisions of this act, as hereinafter specified. (Source: Laws 1897, p. 177.) (805 ILCS 30/3) (from Ch. 32, par. 401) Sec. 3. All gas companies which are authorized to manufacture and distribute illuminating gas shall have the power to manufacture and distribute gas for fuel purposes and to distribute natural gas, and all companies authorized to distribute gas in any city, town or village shall have the power to distribute the same in any territory annexed to such city, town or village. (Source: Laws 1897, p. 177.) (805 ILCS 30/4) (from Ch. 32, par. 402) Sec. 4. The purchase and sale or lease, or the consolidation and merger, hereby authorized, may be made in the manner following: The respective boards of directors or trustees of the company or companies proposing to sell and lease its or their property, and of the company proposing to purchase or lease the same, as under section 1, or of the companies proposing to consolidate and merge into single corporation, as under section 2, may enter into and make an agreement, or agreements, under their respective corporate seals, for such sale or purchase or lease, or for such consolidation and merger, describing the terms and conditions thereof and the mode of carrying the same into effect. (Source: Laws 1897, p. 177.) (805 ILCS 30/5) (from Ch. 32, par. 403) Sec. 5. Before taking effect, the agreement or agreements for sale and purchase or lease, or for such consolidation and merger, authorized by this act, shall be submitted to any annual meeting or to a special meeting of the stockholders of each of the companies. Such special meeting shall be called by delivering personally, or depositing in the post
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces a contradiction by explicitly allowing the vendor to share confidential information with subcontractors 'regardless of a need to know,' while simultaneously restricting disclosure to employees with a 'need to know.' This creates a potential conflict with the intent to protect confidential information. The Law Explanation suggests that this could enable misappropriation of trade secrets if subcontractors are not bound by confidentiality agreements, conflicting with the goal of safeguarding confidential data. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 pertains to gas companies and is not directly relevant, the Law Explanation highlights the risk of unauthorized disclosure, which is a key concern in trade secret protection and confidentiality agreements.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of this Agreement with respect to the disclosure and use of CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION shall survive for a period of three (3) years from the date of last disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of this Agreement with respect to the disclosure and use of CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION shall survive for a period of three (3) years from the date of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'date of last disclosure' to 'date of this agreement' significantly reduces the protection period, especially if confidential information is disclosed later in the relationship. This weakens the protection of confidential information because the 3 year survival period starts at the signing of the agreement, not the actual last disclosure.
perturbation.law_explanation: Limiting the survival period from the date of agreement, rather than the date of last disclosure, provides a shorter window for protecting trade secrets, increasing the risk of misappropriation after the agreement expires, potentially violating the Illinois Trade Secrets Act.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'date of last disclosure' to 'date of this agreement' significantly reduces the protection period for confidential information, creating a legal contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation confirms this weakens protection as the survival period starts at signing, not the actual last disclosure. While the provided law snippet does not explicitly mention trade secrets or confidential information, the Law Explanation states this change increases the risk of misappropriation after the agreement expires, potentially violating the Illinois Trade Secrets Act. Given the explanation that this change shortens the protection window, the potential for violating trade secret laws exists. Therefore, the change introduces a contradiction by potentially undermining legal protection for confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire understanding between the parties hereto as to the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION and merges all prior discussions between them relating thereto.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire understanding between the parties hereto as to some of the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION and merges all prior discussions between them relating thereto.
perturbation.explanation: By inserting the word 'some', the agreement no longer encompasses the entire understanding regarding all Confidential Information. This creates a loophole where prior unwritten agreements or understandings about confidential information not covered by this agreement might be enforceable. It creates ambiguity as to which Confidential Information the agreement covers.
perturbation.law_explanation: By limiting the agreement to 'some' confidential information, it weakens the overall protection of trade secrets, potentially leaving certain information vulnerable to misappropriation and outside the scope of the Illinois Trade Secrets Act's protections.

perturbation.justification: The modification, by limiting the agreement to 'some' confidential information, introduces ambiguity and potential loopholes that undermine the original intent of protecting all confidential information. The changed text creates a scenario where prior unwritten agreements or understandings about confidential information not covered by this agreement might be enforceable. While the provided law snippet does not directly address confidentiality agreements, the Perturbation and Law Explanations highlight how limiting the scope of confidential information weakens its overall protection. The modified clause introduces a potential legal challenge as to what constitutes 'some' versus 'all' confidential information, making the contract less enforceable in its entirety.
